answer: The house was built by Bob.
reasoning: I (past) + hangaia (built-passive) + te whare (the house) + e Bob (by Bob)

answer: The boy spoke to the girl.

answer: The bull will chase the boys.

answer: Kua horoia te pūru e koe.

answer: Nā te tama te mahi i mahi.
reasoning: Emphatic construction: Nā (emphatic past) + te tama (the boy) + te mahi (the work) + i mahi (did)

answer: I haere te wahine ki te whare.

answer: Māori uses different particles to mark tense, aspect, and emphasis. Regular sentences follow [Tense marker] [Subject] [Verb] [Object] order. Passive constructions use [Tense] [Verb-passive] [Patient] [e Agent]. Emphatic sentences use Nā (past) or Mā (future) before the emphasized agent, following the pattern [Nā/Mā] [Agent] [Patient] [i/e] [Verb]. This construction highlights who performed the action, similar to cleft sentences in English. Negative sentences use Kāore with different structures for past (Kāore...i) and future (Kāore...e).
reasoning: Explanation of the grammatical patterns observed in Māori, particularly focusing on emphatic constructions which are the key feature of this problem
